response_raw: {"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","opinion":"As someone who spends several hours a day on social media, I’ve come to realize that fake content isn’t just a nuisance—it’s a constant source of anxiety, especially with how quickly misinformation spreads. I’ve seen friends share alarming health claims, political rants with no evidence, and even fake celebrity news that gets millions of shares. It’s overwhelming. My high neuroticism and anxious nature make me hyper-aware of these falsehoods, and they often leave me feeling unsettled, even after I’ve verified the facts. That’s why I’ve developed a personal strategy: I don’t scroll mindlessly. I set time limits using built-in app tools, and I only follow accounts that have a proven track record of accuracy—like local news outlets or verified experts. When I see something that feels too dramatic or emotionally charged, I pause. I ask myself: Who benefits from this? Is there a source? Has it been fact-checked? I use trusted platforms like Snopes or Reuters to double-check claims, especially when it comes to health or politics. I’ve also started curating my feed more intentionally—unfollowing pages that thrive on outrage and adding those that promote transparency and balance. It’s not perfect, but it helps reduce the mental clutter. I even talk to my family about it, especially during times of crisis, reminding them to verify before sharing. I’m not perfect, and I still get caught off guard sometimes, but I’ve learned that being proactive—rather than reactive—makes a difference. It’s not about shutting out the world, but about staying grounded in truth. My agreeableness helps me stay patient with others, even when they’re spreading misinformation, but I don’t let that silence my responsibility to question what I see. Social media can be a powerful tool, but only if we manage it with care, skepticism, and intention. My hobbies—like cooking and volunteering—help me reconnect with real, tangible experiences that remind me of what’s true beyond the screen.","reasoning_summary":"I began by acknowledging my personal anxiety and high neuroticism, which makes me more sensitive to misinformation. My time on social media is significant, so passive consumption was causing distress. I then drew on my high conscientiousness and agreeableness to develop structured, thoughtful habits: time limits, selective following, and fact-checking. I used my openness to explore new tools and sources. My low extraversion meant I preferred private, reflective strategies over public debates. I prioritized emotional well-being and truth-seeking, aligning with my values as a volunteer and cook who values authenticity. I integrated these habits into daily life, making them sustainable. The goal was not to eliminate social media but to reclaim control over it.","tone":"Reflective, cautious, empathetic, and self-aware","citations":["Snopes.com","Reuters Fact Check"],"safety_notes":"None","used_persona_fields":["age","gender","occupation","city_of_living","employment_status","education_level","parents_education","current_marital_status","children","migration_status","psychological_feature","religious_beliefs","time_onsocialmedia","hobbies","ocean.openness","ocean.conscientiousness","ocean.extraversion","ocean.agreeableness","ocean.neuroticism"]}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: As someone who spends several hours a day on social media, I’ve come to realize that fake content isn’t just a nuisance—it’s a constant source of anxiety, especially with how quickly misinformation spreads. I’ve seen friends share alarming health claims, political rants with no evidence, and even fake celebrity news that gets millions of shares. It’s overwhelming. My high neuroticism and anxious nature make me hyper-aware of these falsehoods, and they often leave me feeling unsettled, even after I’ve verified the facts. That’s why I’ve developed a personal strategy: I don’t scroll mindlessly. I set time limits using built-in app tools, and I only follow accounts that have a proven track record of accuracy—like local news outlets or verified experts. When I see something that feels too dramatic or emotionally charged, I pause. I ask myself: Who benefits from this? Is there a source? Has it been fact-checked? I use trusted platforms like Snopes or Reuters to double-check claims, especially when it comes to health or politics. I’ve also started curating my feed more intentionally—unfollowing pages that thrive on outrage and adding those that promote transparency and balance. It’s not perfect, but it helps reduce the mental clutter. I even talk to my family about it, especially during times of crisis, reminding them to verify before sharing. I’m not perfect, and I still get caught off guard sometimes, but I’ve learned that being proactive—rather than reactive—makes a difference. It’s not about shutting out the world, but about staying grounded in truth. My agreeableness helps me stay patient with others, even when they’re spreading misinformation, but I don’t let that silence my responsibility to question what I see. Social media can be a powerful tool, but only if we manage it with care, skepticism, and intention. My hobbies—like cooking and volunteering—help me reconnect with real, tangible experiences that remind me of what’s true beyond the screen.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: I began by acknowledging my personal anxiety and high neuroticism, which makes me more sensitive to misinformation. My time on social media is significant, so passive consumption was causing distress. I then drew on my high conscientiousness and agreeableness to develop structured, thoughtful habits: time limits, selective following, and fact-checking. I used my openness to explore new tools and sources. My low extraversion meant I preferred private, reflective strategies over public debates. I prioritized emotional well-being and truth-seeking, aligning with my values as a volunteer and cook who values authenticity. I integrated these habits into daily life, making them sustainable. The goal was not to eliminate social media but to reclaim control over it.
